title: Signatures of Quantum Gravity In Relativistic Quantum Systems
summary: In this thesis, we have used a linearized quantum gravity setting to investigate the effects of gravitons on matter systems. Based on the graviton-matter interaction, we have then proposed detector models that may be able to pick up graviton-induced signatures in a matter of a few years. We start with the simple model of a two-particle model detector system interacting with quantized gravitational fluctuations while the detector degrees of freedom obey the generalized uncertainty principle (GUP). For the first part we have hinted at the existence of quantum gravity induced memory effect as well as obtained a quantum gravity modified uncertainty relation. For the next part of the thesis, we have mainly focused on the phenomenological aspects of a linearized quantum gravity theory. For the initial phenomenological model, we have considered the same two-particle model detector system interacting with gravitational fluctuations where the entire set-up is placed inside a harmonic trap potential and looked at the stimulated absorption and spontaneous emission scenario for gravitons. In order to inspect a more involved phenomenological aspect, instead of the standard matter-detector system, we make use of a relativistic scalar Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) and investigated the response of the BEC based model towards incoming gravitons and proposed a graviton detector based on graviton mediated decoherence from entangled BECs.

AI.tldr: The thesis explores graviton-matter interactions using linearized quantum gravity and GUP, proposing detector models for graviton detection through simplified systems and BEC-based approaches.
AI.motivation: To investigate the detectability of gravitons and quantum gravity effects by developing feasible detector models that can potentially observe graviton signatures within a few years.
AI.method:
1. Used a two-particle detector model interacting with quantized gravitational fluctuations under generalized uncertainty principle (GUP).
2. Studied quantum gravity-induced memory effects and modified uncertainty relations.
3. Analyzed a two-particle system in a harmonic trap for graviton stimulated absorption/emission.
4. Proposed a relativistic scalar BEC-based detector leveraging graviton-mediated decoherence from entangled BECs.
AI.result: Demonstrated the viability of proposed detector models, showed quantum gravity effects like modified uncertainty relations, and explored graviton interactions in both simple and complex systems (BECs).
AI.conclusion: The developed models provide promising pathways for experimental detection of gravitons and further exploration of quantum gravity phenomena, emphasizing the role of BEC systems in future gravitational wave and particle detection.

title: Quantum-Deformed Phase-Space Geometry and Emergent Inflation in Effective Four-Dimensional Spacetime
comment: 43 pages, 0 figures, prepared for and submitted to Reports on Progress in Physics (ROPP)
summary: A phase-space approach to quantum-deformed gravity is developed. Following its reduction to an effective four-dimensional spacetime structure, we utilize it in reanalyzing the cosmic inflationary dynamics and quantum gravity. The construction starts on cotangent bundle, where the gravitational Hamiltonian is deformed by a zero-homogeneous scalar determined by projective momentum directions and quantum phase-space properties. This induces an anisotropic Hamilton geometry on a non-null conic domain, from which an effective spacetime metric is obtained through a section-pullback procedure. In the homogeneous and isotropic sector, the pullback consistently reduces to a conformally deformed FLRW geometry governed by a scalar deformation field. We derive the corresponding modified Einstein, Klein-Gordon, geodesic-deviation, and Raychaudhuri equations. This allows for the construction of inflationary background dynamics, slow-roll regime, number of e-folds, as well as scalar and tensor perturbations. The resulting framework shows that leading inflationary corrections arise from the phase-space deformation and its time dependence, while the canonical quantization of cosmological perturbations remains standard after suitable background redefinitions. In this way, the model provides a covariant and controlled link between quantum-deformed phase-space geometry and effective four-dimensional inflationary dynamics. The present construction shows that effects of quantum gravity can be consistently encoded as a deformation of projective phase-space geometry, from which an effective spacetime metric emerges only after a section-dependent reduction.

AI.tldr: A phase-space approach to quantum-deformed gravity is used to re-analyze cosmic inflationary dynamics and quantum gravity, resulting in a conformally deformed FLRW geometry and modified equations (Einstein, Klein-Gordon, etc.). The framework links quantum phase-space deformations to inflationary dynamics, showing that quantum gravity effects manifest as geometric deformations with observable corrections.
AI.motivation: To explore how quantum gravity effects can be encoded in effective spacetime geometry through phase-space deformations, addressing challenges in reconciling general relativity with quantum mechanics during cosmic inflation.
AI.method: Starting with a cotangent bundle, the gravitational Hamiltonian is deformed using a zero-homogeneous scalar based on projective momentum directions and phase-space properties. This induces an anisotropic Hamiltonian geometry on a non-null conic domain. An effective spacetime metric is obtained via section-pullback. The analysis focuses on the homogeneous/isotropic sector, yielding modified equations and inflationary dynamics.
AI.result: The model derives modified Einstein, Klein-Gordon, geodesic-deviation, and Raychaudhuri equations in the FLRW framework. Inflationary parameters like e-folds and perturbations are computed, showing leading corrections from phase-space deformations. Canonical quantization remains intact post-redefinition. The approach provides a covariant, controlled framework linking quantum geometry deformations to cosmological observables.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-deformed phase-space geometry can consistently generate effective inflationary dynamics with measurable gravitational effects. This offers a new pathway to study quantum gravity influences on early universe cosmology through geometric deformations and covariant methods.

title: Hawking area law in quantum gravity
summary: We show that the LIGO--Virgo--KAGRA (LVK) verification of Hawking area law carries profound consequences for quantum gravity if such a law is postulated to hold exactly. The observed mergers can be produced in local Stelle gravity and in nonlocal quantum-gravity theories with entire or fractional form factors either by (i) singular Ricci-flat black holes or (ii) possibly regular classical black holes under very restrictive conditions: absence of $R^2$ and (Riemann)${}^2$ terms in the action, absence of extra real poles in the graviton propagator, and positivity of its spectral representation. To date, this is the strongest simplification of the ambiguities of this class of theories. We also prove that the classical standard black-hole entropy-area law holds as a consequence of Hawking area law, and provide a rigorous realization of Barrow's fractal black holes otherwise.

AI.tldr: The study demonstrates that the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA verification of the Hawking area law has significant implications for quantum gravity theories, particularly in Stelle gravity and nonlocal quantum gravity models. It shows that observed black hole mergers can be explained under specific conditions involving singular or regular black holes, leading to substantial simplification of theoretical ambiguities. Additionally, the work confirms the classical entropy-area law linked to the Hawking area law and introduces a rigorous framework for Barrow's fractal black holes.
AI.motivation: To explore the consequences of the Hawking area law's validation by LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA on quantum gravity theories, addressing ambiguities in Stelle gravity and nonlocal models by imposing observational constraints from black hole mergers.
AI.method: The analysis examines how the exact Hawking area law observed in gravitational wave events constrains the permissible forms of quantum gravity theories. It considers scenarios with singular Ricci-flat black holes or regular black holes under restrictive conditions (e.g., no $R^2$ or (Riemann)^2 terms, positive spectral representation).
AI.result: The findings narrow down allowed quantum gravity theories by requiring adherence to specific structural conditions. They confirm the classical entropy-area law and propose a rigorous model for fractal black holes when the area law is strictly enforced.
AI.conclusion: The LVK observations provide critical constraints on quantum gravity theories, particularly Stelle gravity and nonlocal models, by enforcing the Hawking area law. This resolves ambiguities in these theories and establishes new frameworks for understanding black hole entropy and fractal structures under strict physical conditions.

title: Beyond Three Terms: Continued Fractions for Rotating Black Holes in Modified Gravity
summary: Black-hole ringdown offers a clean probe of strong gravity, but one of its most accurate tools--Leaver's continued-fraction method--requires a three-term recurrence relation. Beyond general relativity, and more generally in non-Kerr spacetimes, Frobenius expansions of the perturbation equations generically produce higher-order recurrence relations and, often, couplings among the series coefficients, obstructing a direct application of Leaver's method. Here we develop a general reduction scheme that maps arbitrary scalar and matrix $N$-term recurrence relations to a three-term form, thereby extending continued fractions to a broad class of perturbation problems in modified gravity. As an application, we compute the quasinormal-mode spectrum of slowly-rotating black holes in dynamical Chern-Simons gravity, where the polar sector yields a 16-term, decoupled, scalar recurrence relation, and the axial sector yields a 12-term, coupled, matrix recurrence relation. After applying our reduction scheme, both systems can be solved with continued fractions. For the fundamental $(\ell,m)=(2,2)$ mode, our results agree well with independent calculations based on eigenvalue-perturbation and metric/spectral methods across the parameter range studied. This framework provides a robust and practical route to precision ringdown calculations beyond the standard three-term setting and supports tests of gravity with current and future gravitational-wave observations.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method to reduce higher-order recurrence relations from non-Kerr spacetime calculations to the three-term form required by Leaver's continued-fraction method, enabling accurate quasinormal-mode calculations in modified gravity theories like dynamical Chern-Simons gravity.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of Leaver's method in non-Kerr spacetimes where higher-order recurrences and coefficient couplings arise, allowing precision ringdown studies in modified gravity.
AI.method: Developed a general reduction scheme transforming N-term scalar/matrix recurrence relations into three-term forms. Applied this to compute quasinormal modes for slowly-rotating black holes in dynamical Chern-Simons gravity, managing both decoupled 16-term scalar and coupled 12-term matrix cases.
AI.result: Successfully applied the reduction scheme to reproduce reliable quasinormal-mode spectra matching independent methods (eigenvalue-perturbation and metric/spectral), valid across studied parameters for $(ξllen,m)=(2,2)$ modes.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables precision ringdown calculations beyond standard cases, providing a robust tool for testing gravity theories using gravitational-wave observations.

title: Inflation from a Weyl-flat null origin
summary: We show that a Weyl-flat null origin of inflation need not be in tension with present observations. For canonical single-field inflation, any background with $ε(N)\to ε_\infty\in(0,1)$ as $N\to\infty$ is asymptotically power-law, inherits the same Weyl-flat null past boundary, and reconstructs an exponential tail in field space. This identifies the origin as an asymptotic universality class rather than a rigid exact solution. We study a minimal deformation, $ε(N)=ε_\infty+(1-ε_\infty)\left(\frac{N_0}{N+N_0}\right)^p$ with $p>1$, which preserves the asymptotic geometry, yields a smooth exit, and produces realistic finite-$N$ phenomenology. Solving the scalar and tensor mode equations directly in e-fold time, we find a viable corridor with $n_s$ in the Planck-preferred range and $r\sim10^{-3}-10^{-2}$, including reheating-compatible benchmarks. The result is a calculable single-field framework in which a Penrose-compatible Weyl-flat inflationary origin survives as a realistic and testable possibility.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that a Weyl-flat null origin of inflation can align with current observations by studying asymptotically power-law inflationary models. It proposes a minimal deformation of the inflationary parameter ε(N) ensuring a smooth exit and realistic cosmological predictions, including viable tensor-to-scalar ratios and reheating scenarios.
AI.motivation: To resolve tensions between a Weyl-flat inflationary origin and observational data, addressing how such a geometric condition can persist while producing measurable outcomes.
AI.method: Analysis of canonical single-field inflation models where ε(N) approaches a constant ε_∞. Introduces a parametrized deformation of ε(N) to maintain asymptotic geometry, solve mode equations in e-fold time, and derive phenomenological predictions.
AI.result: Identifies a viable parameter corridor with scalar spectral index n_s in Planck ranges and tensor-to-scalar ratios r between 10^{-3}-10^{-2}, demonstrating compatibility with observations including reheating. Establishes the Weyl-flat origin as an asymptotic universality class.
AI.conclusion: A Penrose-compatible Weyl-flat inflationary start remains viable and testable through cosmological observations, providing a calculable single-field framework for future study.

title: Asteroid Mining to Sustain a Mars Colony: A Logistics Point of View
comment: 38 pages, 33 figures. Manuscript originally prepared in 2021
summary: Asteroid mining can become an enabling technology to establish a sustainable manned colony on Mars, which requires metallic materials more often than they are readily available in shipments from Earth. This paper describes a feasibility study of a supply chain that delivers metals extracted from metallic asteroids to Mars. The asteroids are selected to respect the $ΔV$ limits imposed by up-to-date spacecraft. The study is conducted with reference to the state of the art in space transportation technologies and in-situ resource utilization. A possibility for mining on carbonaceous asteroids to produce the propellant required for return trips is also taken into account. Different supply chains are computed through a multi-objective optimization routine that considers the mission $ΔV$, the mass of extracted metals and the mass of propellant produced on the asteroids. Schedules to visit the asteroids within reach are obtained and the total mass of the delivered material is evaluated for various mining rates. Finally, the use of the metallic material to build habitats and rovers on the Martian soil through additive manufacturing is discussed.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the feasibility of asteroid mining supply chains to support a Martian colony by optimizing mission parameters, in-situ resource utilization, and additive manufacturing.
AI.motivation: Asteroid mining could provide essential metals for a sustainable Mars colony, which can't rely solely on Earth-supplied materials.
AI.method: Multi-objective optimization analyzes supply chains considering delta-V constraints, material extraction, propellant production, and mission scheduling.
AI.result: Optimal asteroid selection and mission schedules are identified, with calculated material delivery masses based on different mining rates.
AI.conclusion: Asteroid mining supply chains are viable for Mars colonization when integrating in-situ resource utilization and additive manufacturing for habitat/rover construction.

title: Probing heartbeat oscillations from the black hole X-ray binary GRS 1915+105 using spectral-timing analysis
summary: GRS 1915+105 is a black hole X-ray binary exhibiting quasi-periodic $ρ$-class ("heartbeat") oscillations with periods of $\sim$50-100 s, thought to arise from radiation-pressure-driven instabilities in the inner accretion disk at near-Eddington luminosities. The coupled disk-corona response across this instability cycle has lacked simultaneous broadband phase-resolved observational constraints. We present phase-resolved spectral and timing analysis using 24 Swift XRT observations (2014-2016; 1-10 keV) and AstroSat SXT+LAXPC data (2017; 0.8-30 keV), dividing each cycle into five phases (three rise, two decay). We find a systematic anti-correlation between inner disk temperature ($T_{\rm in}$) and apparent inner radius ($R_{\rm in}$): $T_{\rm in}$ decreases from $\sim$1.7 to $\sim$1.5 keV as $R_{\rm in}$ increases from $\sim$22 to $\sim$38 km through Phases 1-3, before $R_{\rm in}$ decreases to $\sim$23 km at the burst peak (Phase 4) and $\sim$18 km post-burst (Phase 5). The broadband fits reveal that the coronal electron temperature $kT_{\rm e}$ rises from $\sim$10.5 to $\sim$14.5 keV through Phases 1-3 and drops to $\sim$6 keV after the burst, while Hardness-Intensity and Color-Color Diagrams show clear spectral hysteresis, with Phase 3 appearing softest in XRT/SXT but hardest above $\sim$10 keV in LAXPC. This evolution is consistent with radiation-pressure instability driving the cyclic $T_{\rm in} - R_{\rm in}$ variations, with coronal heating naturally explained by seed photon starvation via the Haardt-Maraschi mechanism as $R_{\rm in}$ increases. Our 0.8-30 keV coverage provides the first phase-resolved characterization of both the thermal disk and Comptonized corona within a single $ρ$ cycle, directly revealing the disk-corona coupling that drives the heartbeat oscillation and is inaccessible to narrow-band observations alone.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a phase-resolved spectral analysis of GRS 1915+105's ρ-class oscillations using multi-instrument data, revealing systematic correlations between disk and corona properties during the cycle.
AI.motivation: To understand the coupled disk-corona response during radiation-pressure-driven instabilities at near-Eddington luminosities in black hole X-ray binaries, addressing the lack of simultaneous broadband observational constraints.
AI.method: Analyzed 24 Swift XRT observations (2014-2016; 1-10 keV) and AstroSat SXT+LAXPC data (2017; 0.8-30 keV) by dividing each heartbeat cycle into five phases. Performed phase-resolved spectral fitting to study temperature, radius, and coronal properties across these phases.
AI.result: Found anti-correlation between inner disk temperature and radius across phases 1-3, with coronal electron temperature peaking pre-burst and dropping post-burst. Spectral hysteresis observed in Hardness-Intensity and Color-Color diagrams. Broadband data shows disk-corona coupling consistent with radiation-pressure instability models.
AI.conclusion: Confirmed that radiation-pressure-driven instability explains the ρ-oscillation cycle, with Haardt-Maraschi mechanism linking disk expansion to coronal heating. Broadband phase-resolved observations are critical for understanding disk-corona interactions in such systems.

title: String-inspired Gauss-Bonnet Gravity Inflation and ACT
summary: In this article we present a systematic observational verification of the ghost-free string-inspired $f(R,\mathcal{G})$ model, where the Gauss-Bonnet invariant is non-minimally coupled to an auxiliary scalar field $χ$ through the coupling function $h(χ)$. Previous studies confirmed the theoretical viability of this framework using phenomenological parameter choices. In this work, for the first time, a systematic comparison with observational data from Planck 2018 and the Atacama Comsology Telescope is carried out via a Bayesian MCMC analysis using the Cobaya code. We explore an extended set of sixteen models constructed from four types of the Hubble parameter combined with power-law, exponential, hybrid, and inverse logarithmic coupling functions $h(χ)$. The hybrid coupling $h(χ) = γe^{b_1χ}χ^{b_2}$, introduced in this context, allows for interpolation between the power-law and exponential forms, providing additional flexibility in controlling the Gauss-Bonnet contribution at different stages of inflation. All sixteen models reproduce the red spectral tilt of scalar perturbations consistent with CMB observations, yielding $n_s \approx 0.97$ at $N = 60$ e-folds. We find that the preference for the dataset is systematically determined by the choice of Hubble parametrization rather than by the coupling function. The parameter $μ\approx0.1$ remains stable in all configurations, suggesting its fundamental role within the ghost-free formalism.

AI.tldr: The study systematically verifies a ghost-free string-inspired f(R,G) model using observational data from Planck 2018 and the Atacama Cosmology Telescope via Bayesian MCMC analysis. Sixteen models with varying Hubble parameters and coupling functions (power-law, exponential, hybrid, inverse logarithmic) were tested. Results showed that Hubble parametrization choice, not coupling function, determines model preference, with consistent red tilt ns ≈0.97 and stable μ≈0.1.
AI.motivation: To validate the theoretical viability of the string-inspired f(R,G) model with non-minimal Gauss-Bonnet coupling through observational data analysis, moving beyond previous phenomenological parameter choices.
AI.method: A Bayesian MCMC analysis using Cobaya code compared sixteen models (four Hubble parameter types × four coupling functions) against Planck 2018 and ACT data. The hybrid coupling function allowed interpolation between power-law and exponential behaviors.
AI.result: All models reproduce ns≈0.97 at N=60 e-folds. Hubble parametrization choice, not coupling function, drove dataset preference. Parameter μ≈0.1 remained stable across configurations.
AI.conclusion: The Hubble parametrization is critical for model success in observational fitting. The stable μ value suggests its essential role in the ghost-free framework. The hybrid coupling offers flexibility without improving observational preference over other functions.

title: The influence of the inverse Compton effect on the transverse momentum spectra of particles produced in pp collisions at \sqrt{s}=14 TeV
summary: The influence of the QED-analog of the inverse Compton effect on the transverse momentum spectra of particles produced in proton-proton collisions at energies of \sqrt{s}=14 TeV has been investigated. The analysis is based on the quark-gluon scattering process g + q --> g + q, which is the QCD analogue of Compton scattering of a photon on an electron and can lead to energy redistribution between partons, analogous to the mechanism of the inverse Compton effect. Data obtained numerically using the PYTHIA event generator (version 8.316) were used. A total of 5*10^5 proton-proton collisions at \sqrt{s}=14 TeV were analyzed. Events were classified based on the relative energies of the initial quark and gluon, which allowed us to distinguish Compton scattering (DCE) events from inverse Compton scattering (ICE) events. Particle transverse momentum spectra were obtained in the region: p_{T} < 10 GeV/c. The results showed that including inverse Compton scattering events in the analysis leads to a moderate increase in particle yield. The ratio of the spectra for ICE and DCE events remains approximately constant and is about 1.1 within statistical errors. No significant broadening of the transverse momentum spectra is observed. These results show that proton-proton collisions can serve as a reliable baseline for studies of energy redistribution mechanisms in a dense QCD medium, such as quark-gluon plasma.

AI.tldr: The study investigates the impact of the inverse Compton effect's QCD analog (ICE) on particle transverse momentum spectra in 14 TeV proton-proton collisions using PYTHIA. While ICE increases particle yield slightly (by ~10%), it doesn't significantly broaden the spectra. Results suggest these collisions can serve as a baseline for studying energy redistribution in QCD mediums.
AI.motivation: To assess how QCD inverse Compton scattering affects particle production and transverse momentum distributions, providing foundational data for understanding energy redistribution in extreme conditions like quark-gluon plasma.
AI.method: Simulated 500,000 proton-proton collisions at 14 TeV with PYTHIA 8.316, focusing on g+q → g+q processes. Events were categorized into Compton (DCE) vs inverse Compton (ICE) based on initial parton energies. Analyzed pT spectra up to 10 GeV/c.
AI.result: Including ICE events increased particle yields by ~10% (ratio ICE/DCE ≈1.1). No significant transverse momentum broadening observed. Spectral shape differences remain small within errors.
AI.conclusion: Proton-proton collisions with ICE contributions provide a stable baseline for studying energy redistribution mechanisms in dense QCD media, as the effect is measurable but doesn't dominate over background processes.

title: Machine Learning Supports Existence of Previously Unrecognized Transient Astronomical Phenomena in Historical Observatory Images
summary: Transient, star-like point sources that appear and vanish over short timescales are described in astronomical images prior to launch of Sputnik. We have reported that transient numbers diminish significantly in Earth's shadow (shadow deficit) and are more likely within (plus/minus) one day of nuclear testing (nuclear window). These findings remain debated with some arguing that transients identified via existing automated pipelines are simply plate defects. Therefore, we use machine learning (ML) to enhance transient identification accuracy and validate the phenomenon. The model was trained against 250 transient image pairs taken 30 minutes apart that were classified as real versus plate defect by expert visual review; the model demonstrated good discrimination (out-of-fold AUC$=$0.81; sensitivity$=$0.71, specificity$=$0.71). After deployment in a dataset of 107,875 previously-identified transients, the model assigned each a probability of being real. After controlling for ML-identified artifacts, transient counts were significantly elevated for dates within a nuclear window (p$=$.024); transients with the highest probability of being real were more likely to occur within a nuclear window (p$<$.0001). The shadow deficit was significant (p$<$.0001) and largest in the highest probability transients relative to lower probability transients (p$=$.003). Results strongly support existence of an unrecognized population of transient objects in historical astronomical plates warranting further study.

AI.tldr: The paper uses machine learning to validate the existence of transient astronomical objects linked to nuclear tests, showing that these transients are more likely during nuclear test periods and less so in Earth's shadow.
AI.motivation: To confirm whether observed transients are real astronomical events or artifacts, addressing debated claims that existing pipelines might misclassify them as plate defects.
AI.method: A machine learning model was trained on expert-classified transient image pairs (real vs. artifacts) achieving an AUC of 0.81. The model was applied to 107,875 transients to assess their likelihood of being real, with statistical analysis of their occurrence relative to nuclear test dates and Earth's shadow.
AI.result: Transients with higher ML-probability of being real showed significant correlation with nuclear test windows (p<0.0001) and shadow deficit (p<0.0001). even after controlling for artifacts, the correlation remained (p=0.024).
AI.conclusion: The findings confirm the existence of a previously unrecognized transient population in historical plates linked to nuclear events, emphasizing the need for further investigation.

title: Visual Characteristics of a Rotating Black Hole in $4$D Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet Gravity with Thin Accretion Disk Under EHT Constraints
comment: This article contains 32 pages, 25 equations, 13 figures and 82 references
summary: This study investigates the visual characteristics of a rotating black hole (BH) within the fabric of $4$D Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet gravity illuminated with two illumination models, such as a celestial light sphere and a thin accretion disk. To visualize the BH shadow images, we use a recent fisheye camera model and ray-tracing method. And then, we focus on investigating the impact of the coupling parameter $α$ and the spin parameter $a$ on the shadow images. The results exhibit that the shadow radius decreases, while the shadow deviation increases with the aid of $α$. However, with respect to $a$, the shadow radius is slightly increased compared to the corresponding shadow deviation. For a celestial light sphere, the increasing values of $α$, lead to a decrease in the corresponding photon ring, while the space-dragging effect becomes more prominent with increasing $a$. For a thin accretion disk, we enhance its inner edge to the BH event horizon, and the particle motion is different in the regions inside and outside the innermost stable circular orbit. The result demonstrates that the shadow becomes progressively asymmetric with $a$, while the overall size of the inner shadow gradually decreases with the variations of $α$. Subsequently, we also investigated the distinct features of red-shift configurations on the disk for both direct and lensed images. Additionally, we used the latest observational data from M87* and Sgr A* to impose certain parameter constraints on $α$; the results depict the consistency of our considering the BH model.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the visual properties of rotating black holes in 4D Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet gravity using fisheye camera models and ray-tracing, focusing on the effects of coupling parameter α and spin parameter a. The study shows how these parameters alter shadow size, deviations, and disk dynamics, validated against M87* and Sgr A* observations.
AI.motivation: To understand how higher-dimensional gravitational effects (via α) and black hole spin (a) influence observable features like shadow morphology and accretion disk dynamics in Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet gravity. Prior work lacked comprehensive analysis of these parameters in 4D models with modern imaging techniques.
AI.method: Combines ray-tracing simulations with a fisheye camera model to analyze black hole shadows under two illumination scenarios: celestial light spheres and thin accretion disks. Parameters α (Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet coupling) and a (spin) are systematically varied to observe their impact on shadow size, asymmetry, photon ring structure, and accretion disk redshift patterns.
AI.result: Key findings include inverse correlation between α and shadow radius/photon ring size while spin a increases shadow asymmetry and mild radius expansion. Disk inner edge dynamics show parameter-dependent motion splits. Observational data from M87* and Sgr A* constrain α values, aligning with theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet gravity's coupling parameter and spin significantly modify black hole shadow observables, providing testable predictions. The study underscores the importance of multi-parameter analysis in gravitational theories beyond general relativity, validating the model against real astrophysical systems.

title: The Flat Critical Branch Between Nariai and Bertotti-Robinson Geometries as a Solution of Cosmological Einstein-Maxwell Theory
comment: 16 pages, To appear in the proceedings of 60 Years in Mathematical Physics: Gurses-Fest issue of Journal of Physics: Conference Series} (JPCS, IOP Publishing)
summary: We analyze a class of product geometries of the form $\mathbb{R}^{1,1}\times Σ_2$ supported by electric, magnetic, or dyonic flux in the Einstein-Maxwell-$Λ$ theory. These spacetimes belong to a unified family of direct products $(dS_2,\mathbb{R}^{1,1},AdS_2)\times Σ_2$ distinguished solely by the sign of the Lorentzian curvature of the two-dimensional factor. We focus on the critical configuration for which the Lorentzian curvature vanishes. At this balance point between the cosmological curvature and the Maxwell stress, the longitudinal geometry becomes exactly flat while the transverse sphere radius is fixed algebraically by the conserved flux. We refer to this geometry as the critical Maxwell flux string: a homogeneous flux-supported geometry curved only in the transverse directions and invariant along a two-dimensional null worldvolume. It represents the algebraic midpoint interpolating between the Nariai $(dS_2\times S^2)$ and Bertotti-Robinson $(AdS_2\times S^2)$ spacetimes. A qualitative structural change occurs precisely at this midpoint. The spacetime is Petrov type-D with constant scalar curvature invariants, placing it in the degenerate Kundt/CSI class. Because the curvature structure reduces any polynomial rank-two tensor to a linear combination of the metric and the Maxwell stress tensor, the same configuration solves a broad class of algebraic higher-curvature gravity theories. In this sense, the critical flux string and its aligned deformations constitute almost universal solutions.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates a critical configuration in Einstein-Maxwell-Λ theory where Lorentzian curvature vanishes, resulting in a flat longitudinal geometry and fixed transverse sphere radius determined by Maxwell flux. This critical Maxwell flux string acts as a midpoint between Nariai and Bertotti-Robinson spacetimes, belongs to the Kundt/CSI class, and serves as a universal solution for higher-curvature gravity theories.
AI.motivation: To explore unified spacetime geometries in Einstein-Maxwell-Λ theory, focusing on the critical balance point between cosmological curvature and Maxwell stress where structural changes occur.
AI.method: Analysis of product geometries ℝ¹,¹×Σ₂ with electric/magnetic/dyonic flux, examining curvature properties and their transition points. Derives conditions for the critical configuration and checks universality across higher-curvature theories.
AI.result: Identifies the critical Maxwell flux string as a Petrov type-D, CSI spacetime with curvature invariants allowing it to solve higher-curvature gravities universally.
AI.conclusion: The critical configuration is a fundamental midpoint geometry with universal applicability, offering insights into spacetime structure interpolations and broader gravity theory solutions.

title: Hunting Sterile Neutrino Dark Matter in the MeV Gap
comment: v1: 12 pages, 4 figures. Comments are welcome
summary: We investigate the sensitivities of upcoming MeV gamma-ray telescopes to sterile neutrino dark matter in the mass range $(0.2-100)\,{\rm MeV}$. Sterile neutrinos in this regime can produce observable photon signals through radiative two-body decays and three-body decays with final-state radiation. We perform a Fisher forecasting analysis incorporating realistic astrophysical background modeling and detector response to derive projected constraints on the sterile neutrino decay rate. We find that future MeV instruments can improve existing limits by several orders of magnitude across a wide region of parameter space. Our results highlight the discovery potential of next-generation MeV telescopes in probing sterile neutrino dark matter.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates how upcoming MeV gamma-ray telescopes can detect sterile neutrino dark matter through their photon decay signals, showing these instruments could significantly improve limits on sterile neutrino parameters.
AI.motivation: Sterile neutrinos in the 0.2-100 MeV mass range may explain dark matter and produce observable gamma-ray signals via decays. Current limits need improvement, so future telescopes' capabilities are assessed.
AI.method: Fisher forecasting analysis with realistic astrophysical backgrounds and detector responses to model decay rate constraints for sterile neutrinos.
AI.result: Future MeV telescopes could enhance existing constraints by orders of magnitude across broad parameter spaces, greatly strengthening evidence for or against sterile neutrino dark matter.
AI.conclusion: Next-generation MeV instruments hold significant potential to discover or rule out sterile neutrino dark matter through their gamma-ray decay signatures.

title: SAGUI: SED-based Segmentation of Multi-band Galaxy Images -- Application to JADES in GOODS-South
comment: Submitted to MNRAS, comments and suggestions are very welcome
summary: We present sagui, a modular framework for the analysis of multi-band imaging data in spatially resolved galaxies, with synergies to integral-field spectroscopy (IFS). Building on the spectro-spatial paradigm introduced by capivara for IFS data, sagui extends this approach to imaging datasets, enabling a coherent, pixel-level treatment of spatial and spectral information across multiple bands. The method follows a two-stage strategy: a starlet-based decomposition is first used to identify and mask spatial structures across multiple scales while suppressing noise, and a spectral-similarity analysis then partitions the image into coherent pixel groups that preserve spectral consistency. In addition to compact and high-contrast structures, the framework incorporates a dedicated statistical treatment, based on a copula transform, to identify and recover faint, diffuse low-surface-brightness components. We demonstrate the method across a diverse range of galaxy morphologies, highlighting its ability to characterize complex spatial structures, including clumps, bars, interacting systems, and low-surface-brightness features. As a case study, we apply it to eleven morphologically diverse galaxies from the James Webb Space Telescope Advanced Deep Extragalactic Survey in the GOODS--South field. sagui is released under an MIT license and is available at https://rafaelsdesouza.github.io/sagui/.

AI.tldr: Sagui is a modular framework that extends the spectro-spatial analysis paradigm from integral-field spectroscopy to multi-band imaging data, enabling coherent pixel-level treatment of spatial and spectral information. It uses a two-stage strategy involving starlet decomposition and spectral-similarity analysis, along with a copula transform for faint features. Demonstrated on diverse galaxies, including JWST data.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified analysis framework for multi-band imaging data in spatially resolved galaxies, complementing integral-field spectroscopy and addressing the need for consistent spatial-spectral analysis across bands, especially for faint, diffuse structures.
AI.method: 1. Starlet-based decomposition to identify spatial structures across scales and mask noise; 2. Spectral-similarity analysis partitions pixels into coherent groups preserving spectral consistency; 3. Copula transform statistically treats faint, low-surface-brightness components. Modular architecture allows integration with IFS analysis tools.
AI.result: Successfully applied to galaxies with various morphologies (clumps, bars, interacting systems, low-surface-brightness features) using JWST/GOODS-South data, demonstrating robust treatment of complex structures and recovery of faint features.
AI.conclusion: Sagui offers a versatile tool for multi-band imaging analysis, improving spatial-spectral coherence and enabling comprehensive studies of galaxy structures. Open-source release supports community adoption for extragalactic surveys.

title: Binary Evolution Can Mimic the Pair-Instability Mass Gap in Black Hole Mergers
summary: The recent O4a release from the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA collaboration, which significantly increased the number of gravitational-wave (GW) detections, reveals features with potentially important astrophysical implications. One notable example is a hint of the so-called pair-instability mass gap. In particular, the observed decline in the number of black holes (BHs) with mass above 45 Msun, together with indications of possibly higher spins for BHs above this threshold, has been interpreted by Antonini et al. and Tong et al. as evidence for pair-instability supernovae. In this work, we investigate whether mass transfer in binary systems can produce BH components' mass distribution that mimics the pair-instability limit. We use both the population synthesis code StarTrack and a simple semi-analytical framework to highlight the impact of mass transfer efficiency on the BH masses. We find that efficient mass transfer (over 50%) during the first Roche-lobe overflow, followed by a highly non-conservative second mass transfer phase, naturally limits the mass of the first-born BH and produces a cutoff that mimics a mass gap. While the upper mass limit for the more massive BH is increased through accretion during the first mass-transfer phase and is ultimately set by the pair-instability limit, the less massive BH is limited to the stripped primary mass. As a result, the fraction of systems in which the less massive BH exceeds 45 Msun is negligible. While the pair-instability mass gap is a plausible interpretation of current GW data, similar features can naturally arise from binary evolution. Future detections will help distinguish between these scenarios. In particular, a predominance of positive effective spins or low-spin events within the gap would challenge the pair-instability interpretation and instead support a binary-interaction origin for high-mass BHs.

AI.tldr: Analysis of gravitational-wave data suggests binary evolution processes, involving mass transfer efficiency, can mimic the pair-instability mass gap observed in black hole masses, implying the need for future observations to distinguish between astrophysical scenarios.
AI.motivation: To determine if mass transfer in binary systems can reproduce the observed decline in black holes above 45 solar masses and the implications for pair-instability supernovae.
AI.method: Used population synthesis code StarTrack and a semi-analytical framework to model mass transfer efficiency impacts on BH masses, focusing on Roche-lobe overflow phases.
AI.result: Efficient first mass transfer followed by non-conservative second transfer creates a BH mass cutoff mimicking the pair-instability gap. Less massive BHs rarely exceed 45 Msun due to stripped primary mass limits.
AI.conclusion: While the pair-instability explanation is plausible, binary interactions also explain the mass gap features. Spin properties in future GW detections will help differentiate the two scenarios.

title: A Lagrangian framework for canonical analysis for the Holst model with $β= 0$
summary:
We perform a canonical analysis of the Holst model for General Relativity, within the framework laid out in arXiv:2401.07307 and arXiv:2010.07725, distinguishing our approach by setting the Barbero parameter to $β=0$ and leaving the lapse and shift functions unconstrained. The $β= 0$ choice is of particular interest because it is viable across all dimensions, providing a necessary foundation for extending the Loop Quantum Gravity formalism beyond $3+1$ dimensions.
  Through field decomposition and the projection of the field equations, we derive a system of 37 equations (10 differential constraints, 21 algebraic constraints, and 6 evolution equations) exactly matching the 37 field components to be determined. Moreover, leaving the gauge unfixed reveals that three equations, which are typically identically satisfied under normal evolution, are actually differential constraints whose triviality depends on specific gauge choices. The resulting framework remains fully consistent with the standard $3+1$ decomposition of the Einstein equations without requiring any constraints on the lapse and shift functions.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the Holst model of General Relativity using a canonical analysis framework, setting the Barbero parameter β=0 and leaving lapse and shift functions unconstrained. This approach yields 37 equations (10 differential, 21 algebraic, 6 evolution) that align with field components, and reveals gauge-dependent triviality in three equations, extending Loop Quantum Gravity's applicability across dimensions.
AI.motivation: To explore the viability of the Holst model in higher dimensions and extend Loop Quantum Gravity formalism beyond 3+1 dimensions by leveraging the β=0 Barbero parameter, which is universally valid across dimensions.
AI.method: The authors perform canonical analysis by applying field decomposition and projecting field equations, following specified arXiv references. They keep the Barbero parameter at β=0 and allow lapse and shift functions to remain unconstrained to maintain gauge freedom.
AI.result: Derivation of 37 equations (10 differential constraints, 21 algebraic constraints, 6 evolution equations) that precisely match the number of field components. Three equations previously considered trivial under standard gauge choices are identified as non-trivial differential constraints dependent on gauge selection.
AI.conclusion: The framework preserves consistency with the standard 3+1 decomposition of Einstein's equations without imposing constraints on lapse/shift functions, offering a viable path to generalize Loop Quantum Gravity to higher dimensions through β=0.

title: Extremely high-energy bremsstrahlung in matter
summary: The theory of bremsstrahlung $e \to eγ$ by extremely high energy electrons passing through ordinary matter has been qualitatively incomplete. We revisit the suppression of bremsstrahlung by the Landau-Pomeranchuk-Migdal (LPM) effect, here accounting for quantum disruption of that effect from pair production. Our analysis covers the full range of ultra-relativistic electron and photon energies (subject to a few simplifying approximations).

AI.tldr: This paper revisits the suppression of bremsstrahlung radiation from ultra-relativistic electrons due to the LPM effect, incorporating quantum disruption from pair production to achieve a complete theoretical framework.
AI.motivation: To address the qualitative incompleteness in the existing theory of bremsstrahlung suppression by the LPM effect, particularly accounting for pair production's quantum effects that were previously neglected.
AI.method: The authors analyze the interplay between LPM suppression and pair production quantum disruption across all ultra-relativistic electron and photon energy ranges, using simplified but comprehensive approximations.
AI.result: A revised theoretical model that quantitatively accounts for both LPM suppression and pair production effects, filling gaps in previous analyses of high-energy electron bremsstrahlung.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating pair production's quantum disruption is essential for accurate predictions of bremsstrahlung radiation in high-energy physics scenarios involving ordinary matter.

title: Design of a mission to measure the shape and substructure of the 511 keV gamma-ray line from the center of the Milky Way
comment: 14 pages, 6 figures, accepted for publication in JATIS
summary: The 511 keV electron-positron annihilation feature near the galactic center has been detected for more than half a century, yet its origin remains a mystery. In this paper, we describe a concept for a balloon-borne 511 keV $γ$-ray mission called the 511-Spectrometer Mission. The mission will use Transition-Edge Sensor (TES) arrays with thick metal absorbers that are thermally coupled to the TES. The strength of the approach is a projected energy resolution of 200 eV Full Width Half Maximum (FWHM) at 511 keV, enabling detailed studies of the shape and substructure of the 511 keV emission from the galactic center region. A first mission equipped with 8,192 $γ$-ray detectors and a fully active shield and collimator could detect the galactic center with ~35 $σ$ statistical significance. We present the mission concept as well as first results obtained with a prototype detector equipped with $1.35\times1.35\times2$ mm$^{3}$ Bi absorbers. The detector has a quantum efficiency of 15% for 511 keV photons in photoelectric effect interactions. In tests with a $^{137}$Cs source, these prototype detectors show an energy resolution of 525 eV FWHM at 662 keV. We end with a discussion of follow-up missions that use coded mask imaging, or use concentrating or focusing optics to scrutinize the sources of 511 keV $γ$-rays on smaller angular scales.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a balloon-borne mission concept (511-Spectrometer) using TES detectors with metal absorbers to study the galactic center's 511 keV emission. The mission aims to achieve high energy resolution (~200 eV at 511 keV) for detailed spectral analysis and has demonstrated prototype performance with 525 eV resolution at 662 keV. Follow-up missions may incorporate imaging techniques.
AI.motivation: The long-standing mystery of the 511 keV electron-positron annihilation feature's origin near the galactic center necessitates a high-resolution spectroscopic mission to uncover substructures and emission characteristics.
AI.method: Uses Transition-Edge Sensor (TES) arrays with thick metal absorbers for superior energy resolution (~200 eV FWHM at 511 keV). The initial mission includes 8,192 detectors, a shield, and collimator for statistical significance. Prototypes with Bi absorbers showcased 15% quantum efficiency and 525 eV FWHM at 662 keV tested with Cs-137.
AI.result: Prototype detectors achieved 525 eV resolution, demonstrating feasibility. The proposed mission could detect the galactic center emission at 35σ significance. Future missions consider coded masks, concentrating optics for improved imaging.
AI.conclusion: The 511-Spectrometer Mission concept is validated by prototype testing, offering a path to resolve the galactic center's 511 keV emission mysteries through high-resolution spectroscopy and future imaging capabilities.

title: The KM3NeT event: a primordial high energy neutrino?
summary: High energy neutrinos can be injected in the early Universe from the decay or annihilation of long lived primordial relics. We analyse the possibility that the ultrahigh energy neutrino event recently observed by the KM3NeT neutrino telescope could have such an origin. This possibility has the advantage of leading to a sharp spectral feature in a way that the neutrino flux can be small at all energies except at the KM3NeT event energy. Thus, along this scenario the tension with null results from other experiments is reduced with respect to the usual power law case analysed by the KM3NeT and IceCube experiments. At such energies and for an emission around the recombination time, interactions of these neutrinos with background neutrinos prove to be relevant and must be determined from the development of a dedicated code. These interactions, as well as final state radiation processes, modify the spectrum. Interestingly, it turns out that the scenario can also leave an imprint in the CMB that could be probed in the near future. Interestingly too, this scenario does not predict an associated $γ$-ray flux beyond observation. All in all we do find that the high energy neutrino could be a primordial high energy neutrino, provided it has been produced around the recombination time or later.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the possibility that a high-energy neutrino event observed by KM3NeT originated from primordial relics in the early universe, proposing a scenario with a sharp spectral feature to reduce tension with other experiments. It considers neutrino interactions during recombination and their impact on CMB, while avoiding detectable gamma-ray fluxes.
AI.motivation: To explain the observed KM3NeT neutrino event without conflicting with null results from other experiments, which typically expect a power-law flux. The motivation is to reconcile the anomaly with cosmological particle physics mechanisms.
AI.method: Analyzing neutrino production via decaying/annihilating primordial relics, calculating interactions with background neutrinos and final state radiation, simulating spectral modifications, and assessing CMB imprints.
AI.result: The scenario can explain the KM3NeT event with a narrow flux peak at its energy, avoiding conflicts with other experiments. Potential CMB signatures and no excess gamma-rays support the model's viability.
AI.conclusion: The high-energy neutrino might be primordial if produced post-recombination, offering a plausible origin consistent with observational constraints while predicting testable CMB effects.

title: Extrinsic geometry and Hamiltonian analysis of symmetric teleparallel gravity
summary: We analyze the properties of foliations in presence of non-metricity, deriving the generalized Gauss-Codazzi relations in full generality. These results are employed to study the teleparallel framework of non-metric geometry, obtaining constraints on the extrinsic and intrinsic tensors. In particular, an extrinsic symmetric two-tensor plays the role of the extrinsic curvature in Riemannian geometry, whereas no other geometric object can induce new dynamical degrees of freedom. Furthermore, we analyze the variational principle in presence of non-metricity, obtaining the boundary terms for the well-posed and well-defined Cauchy problem. Finally, we exploit the previous results to construct the Hamiltonian of the symmetric teleparallel equivalent of General Relativity, providing a proof that this theory shares the same number of degrees of freedom with its Riemannian counterpart.

AI.tldr: The paper generalizes Gauss-Codazzi relations to include non-metricity, analyzes the teleparallel framework's constraints, and establishes the Hamiltonian structure for symmetric teleparallel gravity, showing it matches General Relativity's degrees of freedom.
AI.motivation: To extend geometric frameworks to non-metric theories, ensuring well-posed Cauchy problems and understanding dynamical degrees of freedom equivalence between teleparallel and Riemannian approaches.
AI.method: Deriving generalized Gauss-Codazzi equations under non-metricity, variational analysis for boundary terms, and Hamiltonian construction for symmetric teleparallel gravity.
AI.result: Identified constraints on intrinsic/extrinsic tensors, confirmed symmetric teleparallel gravity has identical degrees of freedom to General Relativity, and established necessary boundary terms for valid initial value problems.
AI.conclusion: Symmetric teleparallel gravity is dynamically equivalent to General Relativity despite different geometric formulations, validated through Hamiltonian analysis and constraint derivation.

title: Flavomon ray tracing in matter gradients
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, plus End Matter and Supplemental Material
summary: Flavor instabilities develop in neutrino plasmas through emission of flavomons, the quanta of flavor waves. We derive the flavomon equations of motion in slowly varying environments, notably the matter gradients of supernovae, and use them to construct a flavomon ray tracing framework. Combined with a quasi-linear description of flavomon growth, we thus develop a new approach to the global evolution of flavor instabilities. As a first application, we show that the growth of neutrino-mass-induced instabilities is slowed down, but not suppressed, by the inevitable matter gradients. Local stability analysis alone cannot gauge the impact of inhomogeneities and instead must be coupled to flavomon ray tracing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a flavomon ray tracing framework to analyze neutrino flavor instabilities in supernova environments, showing matter gradients slow but don't suppress instabilities, requiring combined local and ray tracing analysis.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of local stability analysis in assessing flavor instabilities in inhomogeneous supernova environments caused by matter gradients.
AI.method: Deriving flavomon equations of motion, developing a flavomon ray tracing framework, integrating quasi-linear growth descriptions, and simulating neutrino-mass-induced instabilities.
AI.result: Matter gradients slow down but do not suppress flavor instabilities; local analysis must be coupled with flavomon ray tracing for accurate assessment.
AI.conclusion: Global dynamics of flavor evolution require considering both local instability growth and wave propagation effects via the proposed framework.

title: Pulsar Selection Criteria and Performance Evaluation of Autonomous X-ray Pulsar Navigation Systems
comment: 68 pages, 26 figures, 6 tables; Article accepted for publication in Acta Astronautica
summary: Current space missions primarily depend on Earth-based Guidance, Navigation, and Control (GNC) systems involving human-in-the-loop operations. X-ray pulsar-based navigation offers a promising alternative by using the very precise periodic X-ray emissions from pulsars for fully autonomous state estimation. This study presents a comprehensive analysis of pulsar selection criteria that significantly influence overall navigation performance. Observational data from the NICER mission is used to derive realistic estimates of measurement noise. Key mission-level constraints, including pulsed flux, pulsar visibility, geometric configuration, and long-term timing stability, are integrated into the pulsar selection process, addressing limitations of existing studies. An extended Kalman filter (EKF) is used for onboard spacecraft state estimation. The proposed system is evaluated in two scenarios: a Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite at 600 km altitude and an interplanetary transfer from Earth to Jupiter. Simulation results show that including the Crab pulsar yields position errors below 7 km in LEO and 20 km during interplanetary transfer with an instrument effective area of 200~cm$^2$; however, the Crab's limited timing stability leads to filter divergence after 20 days without timing model updates. In contrast, more stable pulsars enable long-term autonomy but with reduced accuracy. These results highlight the trade-offs involved in pulsar selection for autonomous navigation and the need to balance competing objectives. Overall, this study demonstrates the feasibility of X-ray pulsar-based navigation and marks a key step towards fully autonomous spacecraft operations.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates pulsar selection criteria for X-ray pulsar-based navigation, using NICER data and an EKF for state estimation. Simulations in LEO and interplanetary scenarios show position accuracy within 7-20 km but highlight trade-offs between pulsar stability and precision.
AI.motivation: Current GNC systems rely on Earth-based infrastructure and human intervention. X-ray pulsar navigation offers autonomy, but existing studies lack consideration of key mission constraints like pulsar stability and visibility.
AI.method: Uses NICER mission data to model measurement noise. Integrates pulsed flux, visibility, geometry, and timing stability into pulsar selection. Employs EKF for state estimation, tested in LEO (600 km) and Earth-Jupiter transfer simulations.
AI.result: Crab pulsar provides 7 km accuracy in LEO and 20 km interplanetary but diverges after 20 days. Stable pulsars maintain long-term autonomy with lower accuracy. Demonstrates feasibility of X-ray navigation.
AI.conclusion: Successful autonomous navigation requires balancing pulsar selection trade-offs. Findings advance practical implementation of pulsar-based systems for future missions.

title: SAETASS: Solver for Astroparticle Equation of Transport Analysis in Spherical Symmetry
comment: 38 pages, 17 figures. Submitted to JCAP. SAETASS official repository is available at https://github.com/jmgarciamorillo/SAETASS and the official documentation can be accessed at https://saetass.readthedocs.io/en/latest/
summary: In order to model astrophysical environments characterized by radial stratification, such as supernova remnants or expanding superbubbles; correctly understanding the transport of non-thermal particles in astrophysical plasmas is essential. While large-scale Galactic propagation codes exist, they are often optimized for Cartesian or cylindrical geometries and lack the efficiency of one-dimensional spherically symmetric problems. In this work, we present SAETASS (Solver for Astroparticle Equation of Transport Analysis in Spherical Symmetry), a novel, open-source numerical tool designed to solve the time-dependent transport equation for astroparticles. The solver is built upon a conservative finite-volume framework that ensures exact particle conservation and numerical stability. To manage the interplay between diverse physical processes, SAETASS employs a modular operator-splitting architecture. Radial advection and continuous momentum losses are treated using a second-order, shock-capturing MUSCL-Hancock scheme, while the diffusive operator is integrated via an implicit, batched Crank-Nicolson algorithm. This approach allows for the robust handling of steep gradients, spatial discontinuities and regularity conditions at the origin. We rigorously validate the code through a suite of tests for pure advection, diffusion and losses. Finally, we demonstrate the solver's capabilities by modelling cosmic-ray proton transport in a real astrophysical scenario. Our results successfully recover established steady-state limits while revealing relevant pre-equilibrium temporal dynamics across Kolmogorov, Kraichnan and Bohm diffusion regimes. SAETASS provides the community with a lightweight, flexible tool for investigating particle acceleration and propagation in complex, radially dependent astrophysical environments.

AI.tldr: SAETASS is a new open-source code designed to solve the time-dependent transport equation for astroparticles in spherically symmetric astrophysical environments. It uses advanced numerical methods to handle radial transport processes efficiently, validated through rigorous testing and applied to cosmic-ray proton modeling in supernova remnant scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing Galactic propagation codes are optimized for Cartesian/cylindrical geometries and lack efficiency for 1D spherical problems critical in radially stratified astrophysical systems like supernova remnants or superbubbles.
AI.method: Uses a finite-volume framework with operator-splitting approach: MUSCL-Hancock scheme for advection/losses, implicit Crank-Nicolson for diffusion. Modular architecture ensures conservation, stability, and handling of steep gradients/spatial discontinuities.
AI.result: Code validation through advection/diffusion tests. Applied to cosmic-ray proton transport, successfully recovering steady-state limits and showing pre-equilibrium dynamics across different diffusion regimes (Kolmogorov/Kraichnan/Bohm).
AI.conclusion: SAETASS provides a lightweight, flexible tool for particle acceleration studies in radially dependent environments, enabling detailed temporal analysis and flexibility for diverse astrophysical models.

title: Quantum Spacetime, Quantum Gravity and Gravitized Quantum Theory
comment: 32 pages, presented at the International Conference "Nonlinearity, Nonlocality and Ultrametricity"
summary: General relativity is a background-independent theory of a dynamical classical spacetime geometry. Quantum theory is formulated in a classical spacetime, as an intrinsically probabilistic, contextual theory of non-classical, interfering probabilities, with a fixed Born rule for computing those probabilities. We argue that the quantum nature of spacetime, which includes a non-commutative dual companion to the (observed) classical spacetime, is the reason behind an intrinsically probabilistic and contextual nature of quantum theory, with the fixed Born rule. In quantum gravity, we claim, quantum theory is gravitized into a background-independent structure with dynamical and contextual quantum probabilities. This proposal implies intrinsic triple and higher-order interference in the context of massive quantum probes, which sheds light on string theory and the observed vacuum energy as well as the masses of elementary particles.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that quantum gravity modifies quantum theory by incorporating a non-commutative spacetime structure, leading to dynamic and contextual probabilities, which explains phenomena like higher-order interference and connects to string theory and particle mass issues.
AI.motivation: To address the contradictions between general relativity and quantum mechanics by exploring how quantum gravity might alter quantum theory's probabilistic framework.
AI.method: Argues that spacetime's quantum nature introduces a non-commutative dual to classical spacetime, affecting quantum probabilities' contextuality and dynamics, moving beyond fixed Born rule.
AI.result: Proposes that gravitized quantum theory exhibits dynamic contextual probabilities and higher-order interference, relevant to string theory and vacuum/ particle mass puzzles.
AI.conclusion: Quantum gravity necessitates a reformulation of quantum theory as background-independent, with contextual probabilities, resolving inconsistencies and offering insights into fundamental physics challenges.

title: A new approach to long-lived particle detection at hadron colliders: the $\textsf{DELIGHT-SHIELD}$ concept
summary: We propose a fundamental shift in the search for beyond the Standard Model long-lived particles (LLPs) at high-luminosity hadron colliders by prioritizing physical background suppression over traditional inner tracking. We introduce $\textsf{DELIGHT-SHIELD}$, a dedicated detector design for a 100 TeV Future Circular Collider at a dedicated interaction point for LLP searches. By replacing the inner parts of the detector with a multi-layered composite shield, followed by tracking volumes, we estimate a suppression of Standard Model hadronic and electromagnetic backgrounds by up to seven orders of magnitude analytically. Full Geant4 simulations validate the effectiveness of this design. Although the achieved suppression is somewhat lower than the analytical estimate, primarily due to secondary particle production within the shield, the residual background remains at a level that is manageable for LLP analyses. It can be further mitigated by applying energy thresholds, as well as vertexing and timing cuts in the downstream detector. Benchmarking against dark scalar model, we show that this shielding based detector concept achieves sensitivity to branching ratios as low as $\mathcal{O}(10^{-9})$ for $h\rightarrowφφ$ process under zero background condition $-$ outperforming general-purpose detector baselines. This strategy not only expands the discovery reach for neutral LLPs but also provides a rigorous experimental handle to distinguish new physics from Standard Model punch-through backgrounds. We further discuss a phased implementation at the High-Luminosity LHC as a critical testbed for this novel detection concept.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new detector design called DELIGHT-SHIELD for the 100 TeV Future Circular Collider, focusing on background suppression to enhance detection of long-lived particles (LLPs). This design replaces inner tracking components with layered shields, reducing SM backgrounds by up to seven orders of magnitude, with simulations supporting its effectiveness despite some secondary particle challenges.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of traditional inner tracking methods in suppressing backgrounds for LLP searches at high-luminosity colliders, improving sensitivity to rare decay processes like $h\rightarrowφφ$ and enabling distinction from Standard Model backgrounds.
AI.method: A dedicated detector design (DELIGHT-SHIELD) with a multi-layer shield followed by tracking volumes. Analytical estimations combined with Geant4 simulations were used to evaluate background suppression. Performance was benchmarked against a dark scalar model.
AI.result: The shield reduces SM backgrounds effectively (actual reduction by several orders of magnitude, slightly lower than analytical estimates due to secondary particles). Achieves sensitivity to branching ratios as low as $\mathcal{O}(10^{-9})$ under zero background, surpassing general-purpose detectors. Proposed phased implementation at High-Luminosity LHC.
AI.conclusion: DELIGHT-SHIELD offers a viable strategy to expand LLP discovery potential and discriminate new physics from backgrounds, with the High-Luminosity LHC as a critical testing ground for future applications.

title: The Gamma-Ray Monitor onboard the SVOM satellite
comment: 13 pages, 15 figures, 4 tables, SVOM special issue (RAA), accepted by RAA
summary: The Gamma-Ray Monitor (GRM) is a key scientific payload onboard the Space-based Multi-band Variable Object Monitor (SVOM) satellite, designed specifically for the detection and study of gamma-ray bursts (GRBs). Launched into a 625 km low-Earth orbit on 22 June 2024, GRM serves as a large-area, wide-field-of-view instrument capable of observing the hard X-ray and soft gamma-ray emissions in the energy range of 15 keV to 5 MeV. Its primary scientific objectives include: promptly triggering and localizing GRBs (with particular sensitivity to short-hard GRBs), measuring spectral and temporal properties of bursts, monitoring charged particle fluxes in orbit. GRM successfully detected its first GRB (GRB 240627B) on 27 June 2024, and has since maintained a detection rate of more than 100 GRBs per year. Cross-instrument comparisons with detectors such as GECAM and Fermi/GBM have validated the performance and data quality of GRM. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of GRM instrument design, reliability verification through ground testing, in-orbit triggering and localization algorithms, performance calibration, and preliminary in-orbit results, demonstrating its capability as a versatile gamma-ray all-sky monitor.

AI.tldr: The GRM onboard SVOM satellite is a powerful tool for detecting gamma-ray bursts, with successful initial operations and validation through cross-instrument comparisons.
AI.motivation: To detect and study GRBs effectively, especially short-hard GRBs, and monitor charged particle fluxes in space.
AI.method: GRM uses a large-area, wide-field-of-view design to observe hard X-ray and soft gamma-ray emissions. It employs ground testing, in-orbit algorithms for triggering/localization, and cross-instrument validation with GECAM and Fermi/GBM.
AI.result: GRM detected its first GRB in June 2024 and maintains over 100 annual detections. Cross-comparisons confirmed its performance and data quality.
AI.conclusion: GRM is a reliable all-sky monitor for GRBs, fulfilling its scientific objectives through robust design and validation.

title: Cosmic Ray Electron Evolution in Supernova Remnants: Log-Parabola Distribution
summary: The shock fronts of supernova remnants (SNRs) are believed to be significant sites of acceleration of cosmic ray particles. Previous researchers have shown that a particle distribution similar to a log-parabola can be generated when particles have an energy-dependent escape. We explore the acceleration of electrons at SNR shock fronts, and show that modeling this energy-dependent particle escape model can produce spectral energy distributions consistent with observations of two lepton-radiation-dominated SNRs: RX J1713.7-3946 and SN 1006. The model includes the evolution of both the electron distribution and photon spectra as a result of the combined effects of the SNR evolution and electron energy loss. The electron-escape energy dependence is quite weak, but the electron distribution and photon spectra turn out to be very sensitive to changes in the electron escape. We also explore how sensitive the spectra and electron distributions are to the parameters used in the log-parabola model.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates electron acceleration at supernova remnant (SNR) shock fronts using an energy-dependent particle escape model, showing its consistency with observations of RX J1713.7-3946 and SN 1006. It highlights the sensitivity of electron spectra and photon distributions to escape parameters despite weak energy dependence.
AI.motivation: To understand how SNR shock fronts accelerate electrons and produce observed spectra, especially in lepton-dominated SNRs where energy-dependent escape mechanisms may play a critical role.
AI.method: Developed a model incorporating energy-dependent electron escape, tracking electron distribution and photon spectra evolution under SNR dynamics and energy losses. Tested sensitivity to log-parabola model parameters.
AI.result: The model reproduces observed spectra of RX J1713.7-3946 and SN 1006. Weak electron-escape energy dependence still significantly impacts spectralshapes and electron distributions.
AI.conclusion: Energy-dependent electron escape is crucial for explaining lepton-dominated SNR observations. Future studies should focus on refining escape mechanisms and parameter constraints in log-parabola models.

title: Viability of Big Bang Nucleosynthesis in Some Generalized Horizon Entropies
summary: In this work, we investigate the viability of some cosmological models derived from generalized horizon entropies, using Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN) constraints. By analyzing the deviations in the expansion rate, we derive bounds on the model parameters from freeze-out temperature, helium, and deuterium abundances. Our results show that the freeze-out condition provides the most stringent constraint, while helium and deuterium bounds remain consistent across all models. Although lithium constraints are not satisfied, this discrepancy is attributed to the well-known cosmological lithium problem. Furthermore, the parameter values required for late-time cosmic acceleration are found to lie well within the BBN bounds, demonstrating consistency between early- and late-Universe behavior. These results establish the viability of the considered models within the framework of BBN.

AI.tldr: Investigates cosmological models from generalized horizon entropies using BBN constraints, finding freeze-out temperature constraints are most stringent but late-time acceleration parameters fit within BBN bounds, despite lithium discrepancies linked to the cosmological lithium problem.
AI.motivation: To assess validity of cosmological models derived from generalized horizon entropy through BBN observables, ensuring consistency between early universe conditions and late-time cosmic acceleration mechanisms.
AI.method: Analyzed deviations in expansion rate caused by entropy-based models using BBN constraints like freeze-out temp, helium, deuterium abundances. Derived parameter bounds via these observables.
AI.result: Freeze-out provides strictest constraint; He/D bounds align across models. Lithium inconsistencies persist due to known cosmological lithium problem. Late-time acceleration parameters comfortably reside within BBN-derived limits.
AI.conclusion: Models are viable per BBN analysis as they satisfy key constraints (except lithium), with early/late universe dynamics showing consistency within framework.

title: Extracting Dark-Matter Mass from Angular Scanning
summary: We propose a novel method to determine the mass scale of ambient dark matter, applicable to (at least effectively) two-dimensional direct detection experiments that allow for directionality observables. Due to the motion of the solar system and Earth relative to the Galactic Center and the Sun, the dark-matter flux exhibits a directional preference. We first demonstrate that dark-matter event rates depend non-trivially on the angle between the detection plane and the overall dark-matter flow, with the curvature of this angular spectrum encoding mass information. As proof of principle, we take the recently proposed Graphene-Josephson-Junction-based superlight dark-matter detector as a concrete example and validate these theoretical expectations through numerical analyses.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel method to determine the mass scale of ambient dark matter using directionality observables in two-dimensional direct detection experiments, demonstrated with a Graphene-Josephson-Junction-based detector.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of detecting superlight dark matter by leveraging directional preferences in dark-matter flux caused by the motion of the solar system and Earth, thereby improving mass scale determination.
AI.method: The method involves analyzing the angular dependence of dark-matter event rates relative to the dark-matter flow direction, using the curvature of the angular spectrum to encode mass information. The authors use a Graphene-Josephson-Junction-based detector as a case study for numerical validation.
AI.result: Numerical analyses validate the theoretical expectations, showing that the angular spectrum's curvature effectively encodes dark matter mass information in the proposed detector setup.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a viable pathway for determining the mass scale of superlight dark matter using directional detection techniques, offering a new strategy for future experimental designs.

title: Seeing at Timau National Observatory Based on ERA5 Dataset
comment: 11 pages, 7 figures, This preprint has not undergone peer review or any post-submission improvements or corrections. The Version of Record of this article is published in Experimental Astronomy
summary: Understanding the seeing conditions is crucial for astronomical observations using a ground-based telescope. This study analyzes long-term atmospheric data (2002-2021) from the ERA5 dataset to assess the seeing conditions at the new Timau National Observatory in Indonesia, which will house a 3.8-meter optical telescope. While the ERA5 dataset shows good agreement with radiosonde data for temperature and wind speed, it tends to underestimate seeing at Eltari Airport, Kupang. Despite this discrepancy, the ERA5 data suggest a median seeing of 0.79 arcseconds at Timau, with optimal seeing conditions in March and December and greater variability during the May to September dry season. These findings are crucial for the planning and operation of the observatory, which requires excellent seeing conditions for its three-band optical imager and a near-infrared camera. Although the seeing at Timau is not as good as some other observatories, the conditions at Timau make it an observatory that has good prospects for equatorial regions.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates the seeing conditions at the Timau National Observatory using ERA5 data, finding a median seeing of 0.79 arcseconds with optimal conditions in March and December, positioning Timau as a promising equatorial observatory despite not surpassing top global sites.
AI.motivation: To assess atmospheric conditions for effective planning and operation of the new 3.8-meter telescope at Timau, ensuring suitability for advanced optical and near-infrared observations.
